Name: Colossus 2
Notes: Colossus 2 is the follow-up to Colossus 1. Like its predecessor, it is located in Memphis and was converted from an existing building. To start powering the data center, xAI made the unusual choice to install natural gas turbines across the border in Mississippi, possibly to get faster approval for their operation. We use a combination of satellite/aerial images and announcements to estimate the timeline and compute capacity of the data center.
Selected Sources:
- WSJ profile of the Colossus data centers
- Final GPU count reported by Elon Musk
- MLGW update mentioning power requests for Colossus 2
- SpaceX-Cursor partnership to use Colossus

Name: Microsoft Fairwater Atlanta
Notes: Microsoft Fairwater Atlanta is a QTS-built data center with Microsoft as a tenant, possibly for the purpose of providing compute to OpenAI. We estimate compute for it primarily based on satellite and aerial imagery of the chillers.
Selected Sources:
- 2023 Air Permit application
- YouTube video with aerial views
- Semianalysis presentation
- Bloomberg article

Name: Anthropic-Amazon New Carlisle
Notes: Anthropic-Amazon New Carlisle is part of the Anthropic-Amazon Project Rainier collaboration. It is similar to other Amazon locations, forgoing most external cooling infrastructure in favor of direct air based cooling.
Selected Sources:
- Air Construction Permit application for the northern portion
- Backup diesel generator referenced in the permit
- Announcement of Project Rainier coming online

Name: Microsoft Fairwater Wisconsin
Notes: Microsoft Fairwater Wisconsin is set to be the tech giant’s most powerful data center yet. The buildings have a two-story structure, with GPU racks networked vertically as well as horizontally. Each of these GPU buildings is paired with a one-story building dedicated to data storage and processing using CPUs. We estimated compute capacity based on disclosures from Microsoft, and permit applications showing the layout of server racks. Data on Building 3 and 4 is more uncertain, as it is based on the relative sizing of the buildings and mechanical areas in architectural drawings (see this calculation).
Selected Sources:
- Microsoft blog with engineering details
- Microsoft blog with more general information
- Construction Permit Application for building 1
- Construction Permit Application for building 3 and 4

Name: Meta Prometheus
Notes: Meta Prometheus is unusual for its ad hoc construction, spanning the full length of New Albany Business Park. A combination of weatherproof tents, colocation facilities and Meta’s traditional data center buildings, this data center shows Meta’s pivot towards AI. Reflecting that patchwork, our analysis uses a combination of land use maps, natural gas turbine permitting, and satellite/aerial imagery of cooling equipment to estimate compute capacity.
Selected Sources:
- Meta blog post with details on Prometheus
- New Albany Business Park land use map
- Letter on Socrates South Power Generation Project

Name: QTS Richmond
Notes: QTS Richmond is a 15-building campus with an estimated total IT power over 650 MW. Construction on the first site began in 2022 and was accelerated significantly in 2024. QTS owns enough adjacent land to build two or three additional sites of the same size and has indicated to regulators that this could be a 1GW+ campus by 2030. However, further development of this area will require new power infrastructure to be built by the local grid operator, which is still in the permitting phase.
Selected Sources:
- Site 1 factsheet
- Site 2 factsheet
- Site 3 factsheet
- Environmental permit detailing power generation

Name: Colossus 1
Notes:
Colossus 1 was converted from a factory into a data center in 2024. Initially, Colossus 1 was powered by portable gas turbines and a small pre-existing substation. This infrastructure has since been replaced by two additional substations providing power from the local grid, with batteries providing backup power.

Cooling for the building initially used a mix of cooling towers and portable air-cooled chillers, but enough cooling towers have now been installed to handle the full load.

In May 2026, Anthropic signed an agreement with SpaceX to use all of the compute capacity at Colossus 1.
Selected Sources:
'- May 2025 Electricity Provider Disclosure
- July 2025 Permit for Gas Turbines
- Southern Environmental Law Center Gas Permit Appeal, with additional satellite images
- Disclosure on X (formerly Twitter) by Elon Musk of the breakdown of GPUs
- Anthropic announcement

Name: OpenAI Stargate Abilene
Notes: The Stargate data center campus in Abilene Texas is expected to host OpenAI's largest compute cluster from the second half of 2025 through 2026. This is the first of several sites in the Stargate project, spanning the US and the globe. We have a variety of sources to estimate the capacity of this data center, from company announcements, to permit documents, to satellite imagery of air-cooled chillers.
Selected Sources:
- Crusoe 2024 Impact Report, with details on Abilene
- Air Permit Application for buildings 1-2, featuring blueprints on p.157
- Mortensen’s profile of the project, with details of substations

Name: Google Columbus
Notes: Google Columbus is currently a 5-building campus in Ohio. Power capacity was estimated by factoring in both cooling as well as a permitting in the form of a transmission line construction notice, which gives detailed breakdowns of current capacity and planned capacity. The compute capacity is based on this power capacity and the energy efficiency of Google TPU chips that are available when each building becomes operational.
Selected Sources:
- DCD article detailing early work beginning on the site
- Transmission line construction notice; details current site capacity, expansion, and maximum future site capacity

Name: Amazon Madison Mega Site
Notes: Amazon's Madison Mega Site is similar to other Amazon locations, forgoing most external cooling infrastructure in favor of direct air based cooling. We believe it is plausibly part of the Anthropic-Amazon Project Rainier collaboration, based on the New York Times reporting that there’s at least one such data center in Mississippi, though Amazon's data center in Ridgeland is also there. Analysts at SemiAnalysis have indicated this data center is an Anthropic-Amazon collaboration.
Selected Sources:
- Air Construction Permit
- Backup diesel generator referenced in the permit
- New York Times article on Project Rainier
- SemiAnalysis article with imagery of Canton they have labeled as being an Amazon-Anthropic collaboration
Address: Amazon Data Services Inc, Madison Mega Site Nissan Parkway and Highway 22 Canton, Mississippi Madison County

Name: Google New Albany
Notes: Google’s New Albany data center is one of several data centers in the New Albany Business Park. We only include the buildings added or modified since 2024, when AI-specific capacity is more likely. We estimate the compute capacity primarily based on satellite and aerial imagery of the cooling towers, which are similar to towers documented at Google’s Omaha site.
Selected Sources:
- Draft basis of air permit for Google Omaha, a similar data center
- Google’s 2025 Environmental Report
Address: 1101 Beech Rd SW, New Albany, OH 43054

Name: Microsoft Goodyear
Notes:
The Microsoft Goodyear campus spans six years of construction and operations. Most of its compute capacity comes from Building 3 and 4, which we believe house NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 servers based on the density of cooling equipment. SemiAnalysis reported that this data center was intended for OpenAI use.

We estimate power capacity for Building 3 and 4 based on our air-cooled chiller model, and then calculate a power capacity for Building 2-3 based on the relative proportion of backup diesel generators. Compute capacity is based on the power capacity and the availability of different NVIDIA chips over time.
Selected Sources:
- Maricopa County map showing PHX-10 and PHX-11 buildings owned by Microsoft
- Dust control permit for project titled "MICROSOFT CORPORATION PHX10-11 DATA CENTER OFFSITE IMPROVEMENTS"
- SemiAnalysis article that includes similar estimates and associates the data center with OpenAI

Name: Meta Temple
Notes: Meta Temple is notable for a mid-flight change in construction. A traditional H-shaped Meta data center was partially built on the site by 2023, only to be demolished in favor of Meta's new AI-optimized data center design. The power capacity of the new design was reported by construction company JE Dunn, and is backed up by our cooling model. Later, a third data center building was added to the plan. Compute capacity assumes that NVIDIA GB300 NVL72 servers are used, as these are the most popular AI chips at the time.
Selected Sources:
- JE Dunn project page mentioning a 152 MW power capacity
- TDLR filing reporting the start and end date, square footage of Building 1 and 2
- TDLR filing for Building 3

Name: Google Omaha
Notes: Google’s Omaha data center is their second in Nebraska, following a site in Papillion. We estimate its compute capacity primarily based on satellite and aerial imagery of the cooling towers, which are documented in a draft air permit.
Selected Sources:
- Draft basis of air permit with details of cooling towers
- Google’s 2025 Environmental Report

Name: Alibaba Zhangbei
Notes: Alibaba Zhangbei spans three adjacent sites, built from 2016 to 2025. It is part of China's "Eastern Data Western Compute" (东数西算) project launched in 2021. Zhangbei county is favorable for this project due to its abundant wind and solar power. We only track the power capacity added here since 2024, which is higher-density and more likely for AI workloads. The power capacity is based on the air-cooled chillers seen in satellite images. Compute capacity is mostly calculated from power, based on the estimated energy efficiency of leading hardware available in China.
Selected Sources:
- Sinocities blog on Eastern Data Western Compute sites
- News on the Chahar site, with an aerial photo
- Hebei government page with 3D renderings of the Miaotan North and Chahar sites
- Announcement of the Hebei Zhangbei Intelligent Computing Center, part of this data center
Address: Alibaba Zhangbei Yun Calculation Data Center, Zhangbei County, Zhangjiakou, Hebei, China, 076471

Name: CoreWeave Denton TX
Notes:
This Denton datacenter, currently owned by Core Scientific and formerly a bitcoin mining facility, is currently being repurposed to run AI on CoreWeave's GPUs. The contract states 260 MW total dedicated for CoreWeave, which is scheduled to finish by "Midyear 2026".

Information was primarily pulled from quarterly reports, official Core Scientific articles, among other official stakeholder materials. Satellite imagery was also utilized, but wasn't given too much weight considering the datacenter is only partially contracted to CoreWeave, not the entire thing.
Selected Sources:
- Core Scientific - CoreWeave Announce 1.2B expansion in Denton, TX
- Core Scientific - Denton Datacenter Official Site
- TMF - Core Scientific Q4 2025 Earnings Transcript

Name: Microsoft Project Osmium
Notes: Microsoft Project Osmium, which is now fully completed, is a 5-building campus located in Iowa. We believe only four of the buildings are used for AI, starting with the second completed building containing ~25k A100s used by OpenAI to train GPT 3.5 (per SemiAnalysis reporting). Power capacity was derived from a SemiAnalysis figure from the same report, and was estimated as identical across buildings due to near-identical structures. Although one building has chillers, we don’t believe they cover full cooling capacity. Other cooling methods are likely used in addition.
Selected Sources:
- SemiAnalysis article with IT power estimations and detailing usage by OpenAI for training GPT 3.5
- DCD Article on site expansion
- MISO Announcement reporting power for newest buildings, likely an overestimate

Name: Google Council Bluffs (East)
Notes: Google Council Bluffs dates back to 2014, but our analysis focuses on the buildings added to the east since 2024, which are likely to have most of the AI compute. These buildings are similar to other Google data centers like New Albany and Omaha. We estimate the power capacity based on our cooling tower model. The compute capacity is based on this power capacity, and the energy efficiency of Google TPU chips that are available when each building becomes operational.
Selected Sources:
'- Construction permit application form with site address
- Construction permit application form with backup generator data (believed to be for Building 3)

Name: Google Storey County
Notes: Google Storey County is one of the rare dry-cooled Google campuses, adapting to the water-scarce state of Nevada. Growing slowly since 2019, the campus is now planned to have four data center buildings.
Selected Sources:
'- Air quality permit application listing emergency generators and a small "R&D" fuel cell plant (via Aterio)
- Google data center page

Name: Oracle Batam
Notes: Oracle's campus in Batam, Indonesia is built and operated by DayOne, which has a large presence in Southeast Asia. We use the publicly-reported IT power of the facility (backed up by our cooling model). We then derive compute capacity from this power, assuming the campus is filled with the latest NVIDIA Blackwell servers.
Selected Sources:
- DayOne discloses a partnership with "leading global tech company" at their Batam campus
- Oracle announces their Batam cloud compute region
- Oracle is later reported to be leasing DayOne data centers in Indonesia
- DayOne video stating 72 MW of IT power and NVIDIA partnership, and showing cooling tower fan movement

Name: Fluidstack Lake Mariner
Notes: Built on the shores of Lake Ontario at a former coal plant, the Terawulf-owned Lake Mariner campus serves a mix of crypto-mining and AI computing applications. We only track the AI buildings here. Buildings 1-2 (70 MW) are leased to G42 subsidiary Core42, while Buildings 3-5 (440 MW) are leased to Fluidstack. Our sources point to Fluidstack's buildings serving Google TPUs for Anthropic. We named this site after Fluidstack because it has leased the vast majority of the AI capacity.
Selected Sources:
'- Terawulf press release with details of Core42 lease for Buildings 1-2
- Terawulf press release with details and timeline for Buildings 1-5
- DCD article suggesting that Fluidstack will deploy Google TPUs at Lake Mariner
- Announcement of Anthropic-Fluidstack partnership, including data center(s) in New York

Name: STACK Infrastructure NVA02
Notes:
Stack Infrastructure NVA02 is a 10-building (6 for AI) data center campus, with a future IT power capacity of 350 MW for AI.
Construction began Q4 2019, with their non-AI buildings finishing by Q4 2022.
Construction ramped up in 2023, with the first high-density AI data center (containing two independent halls) becoming operational Q4 2024.
Their progress indicates a 2028 finish date for the six total AI data centers.
Selected Sources:
- Official website for NVA02, showcasing their project.
- Most recent official schematic of the NVA02 campus.
- Air quality permit from the state of Virginia.

Name: Stream Phoenix
Notes:
Stream is a Phoenix-based 7-building campus, with 6 of its projected buildings set to be AI ready.
By 2030, they aim to serve 280 MW (220 MW for AI) of IT power  across the campus.
Selected Sources:
- Official page for the data center.
- Official pre-construction planning document.

Name: Vantage TX1
Notes: Vantage’s TX1 campus is one of two known campuses in the San Antonio area. It is comprised of two buildings: Building TX11, which has already finished construction and is operational, and Building TX12, which has just started construction. Power capacity was determined based on capacity reported in construction permitting and supported by estimation from satellite images. Compute capacity was estimated assuming B200s will be used in both. Based on air quality permits for the site, a third building (TX13) has been planned as well, though there are no signs of construction.
Selected Sources:
- Construction Permitting for TX11
- Construction Permitting for TX12
- Air quality permitting that references TX13

Name: Start Campus Sines Data Campus
Notes: The Start Campus Sines data campus, of which the first building is SIN01, is a data center campus in Portugal. This six building campus is reported to be completed in 2030, with an IT capacity of 1.2 GW. As of right now, only one building has been completed with the second building beginning construction. This existing building is operational and being used to support Microsoft.
Selected Sources:
- Website for the site
- Factsheet for the full site
- Article detailing Microsoft collaboration and GPU amounts
Address: Start Campus - Sustainable Data Center Services, Sines, Portugal

Name: Google Pryor (North)
Notes: Dating back to 2007, Google Pryor serves a variety of uses, from Google Workspace to machine learning. Our analysis only considers those buildings completed since 2024, which are more likely to house modern AI compute. We estimate power capacity using our model of the cooling towers seen in satellite imagery. The compute capacity is derived from the power capacity, based on the energy efficiency of the best available Google TPU chips.
Selected Sources:
- Google announces expansion of Pryor facility
- Google 2025 Environmental Report with PUE and water use on pp.109-110
- News of a 9 exaflop TPUv4 cluster in the Pryor data center, launched in 2022

Name: Meta Hyperion
Notes: Meta Hyperion is built on an enormous tract of land, more than three times the size of New York City’s Central Park. It is set to host Meta’s next flagship AI cluster after Prometheus. We estimate its compute capacity primarily based on an application by Entergy for three 752 MW gas turbines linked to the site, as well as plans for a new 1.8 GW substation by the grid operator MISO.
Selected Sources:
- Data center profile by Meta
- Entergy's application for generation and transmission resources
- MISO update with 1.8 GW substation plan

Name: Goodnight
Notes:
Crusoe's Goodnight data center is notable for a major mid-flight change in design. It was originally designed in 2024 for seven X-shaped buildings that closely mirror the first eight buildings of the OpenAI Stargate Abilene data center (also built by Crusoe). This was revised in 2025 to 6 buildings, with Buildings 3-6 using a simple rectangle shape.

Given the structural similarities to Abilene, it is plausible it is equipped with NVIDIA chips. Google is a potential user, based on reporting that Google is investing in a data center in Armstrong County.

Goodnight is a location near the data center, and it is used for naming in the tax abatement agreements we have inspected. However, the data center is actually located in the town of Claude, Texas.
Selected Sources:
- Amended tax abatement agreement, with updated blueprints on p.46
- Original tax abatement agreement, with original blueprints and specs on the last page
- TABS database, with "Llano" data centers in the city of Claude
- Press release reports Google investing in a new data center in Armstrong County

Name: Amazon Ridgeland
Notes: Amazon Ridgeland is similar to other Amazon locations, forgoing most external cooling infrastructure in favor of direct air based cooling. We believe it is plausibly part of the Anthropic-Amazon Project Rainier collaboration, based on the New York Times reporting that there’s at least one such data center in Mississippi, though Amazon's data center in Canton is also there.
Selected Sources:
- Air Construction Permit
- Permit review document
- Backup diesel generator referenced in the permit
- New York Times article on Project Rainier

Name: OpenAI Stargate UAE
Notes: The Stargate data center campus in Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates, is the first non-US Stargate cluster announced by OpenAI. We estimate power capacity based on company announcements and the scale of buildings in satellite imagery, and also use that imagery to estimate the construction timeline. Compute capacity is based on reported export agreements with the US, which suggest that G42 and/or Oracle can acquire 100,000 state-of-the-art NVIDIA chips each year.
Selected Sources:
'- News update with timelapse of construction
- Announcement of the (separate) 100 MW Khazna Ajman data center, which started earlier and has a similar design to the Stargate buildings.
- News about the US approval of NVIDIA chips in the UAE
Address: Nexus L&T Project Office, Al Bihouth, Al Dhafrah, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates

Name: Coreweave Helios
Notes: Helios is retrofitted from a former crypto-mining facility owned and operated by Galaxy. Now in the AI infrastructure business, Galaxy is expanding the site. Galaxy plans to lease its approved 800 MW of capacity to AI neo-cloud Coreweave. An additional 2.7 GW of capacity is under study, but even if that happens, it is not planned to be operational before 2029. We report the power capacity based on Galaxy's reports, which roughly match permit documents and our cooling model. Compute is estimated based on power capacity and the server rack density reported by Galaxy.
Selected Sources:
- Galaxy Q3 financial results with power capacity timeline
- Permit review with details on backup generators and power capacity
- 3D renderings of Helios Building 1 on Galaxy's website, showing generators and chillers
- TDLR record with Building 1 construction costs and end date

Name: Google Cedar Rapids
Notes: Google's Cedar Rapids data center is similar to earlier Google data centers like New Albany and Omaha. Curiously, permitting suggests it will use more than twice as many cooling towers per building as those data centers, but most of the evidence suggests that the power capacity per building is largely unchanged.
Selected Sources:
- MISO new load announcements, featuring 600 MW for Google in Cedar Rapids
- Permit application detailing the number of buildings, cooling towers, and backup generators.
Address: 5800 EDGEWOOD RD SW CEDAR RAPIDS, IA 52404

Name: QTS Cedar Rapids
Notes: QTS Cedar Rapids sits alongside a Google data center in Big Cedar Industrial Center. It is divided into two phases which are expected to  be completed in late 2026 and late 2027. We estimate its power capacity based on satellite images and 3D renderings of the air-cooled chillers lining each building. The compute capacity is based on this power capacity and the assumption that the best available NVIDIA AI servers will be used for each phase. The estimated power for phase 1 is consistent with a 616 MW load request for a QTS shell company in Cedar Rapids. However, the total capacity across both phases is more than double that, suggesting that QTS will need to acquire more power by 2027 to fulfil their plan.
Selected Sources:
- MISO new load announcements, including 616 MW in Cedar Rapids for a "Shell Corp" called SNA LLC.
- Article by The Gazette featuring a rendering of Building 2 and 3.
- Article by Corridor Business Journal featuring a rendering of Buildings 1-3.
- Post by GLD Commercial featuring a map with land assigned to QTS in Big Cedar Industrial Center.

Name: OpenAI Stargate Shackelford
Notes:
Stargate Shackelford is the second US site in the Stargate project led by OpenAI, Oracle and Softbank. Just 20km from the original Abilene site, this site is developed by data center colocation company Vantage, who refers to it as "Frontier". In September 2025, OpenAI announced a site in Shackelford County, Texas, and Vantage later confirmed their data center is part of Stargate.

The date that this campus reaches full capacity is speculative. However, Vantage states the planned capacity on their website, along with the delivery date of the first building in late 2026. We estimate compute capacity based on the best NVIDIA AI servers expected to be available as each building comes online.
Selected Sources:
- Vantage data center page with power capacity, number of buildings, and first building timeline
- OpenAI Stargate expansion announcement mentioning Shackelford site
- Vantage LinkedIn post connecting the data center to OpenAI Stargate
Address: 175 Private Road 1604, Abilene, TX 79601, Shackelford County

Name: Google Fort Wayne
Notes: Google Fort Wayne will be a five-building campus likely designed to house current and next-gen TPUs. The campus is also notable for being one of the first sites to use the new Google building design, which is larger and has more cooling infrastructure. (There is also the 'H' building which we haven't seen before, but we don't think it's for AI compute.) We estimate that when it's completed in 2028, it will consume approximately 1 GW of total facility power and produce 1.2 million H100-equivalents' worth of compute.
Selected Sources:
- Google's plans for wetlands in Fort Wayne
- Data center's diesel generators
- Project's history and tax abatement
- City Council approval
Address: 6015 Adams Center Road, Fort Wayne, IN 46806

Name: Crusoe Abilene Expansion
Notes: Built by Crusoe and originally planned as an expansion to Stargate Abilene for OpenAI and Oracle, this site reportedly now has interest from NVIDIA and Meta but was ultimately acquired by Microsoft.
Selected Sources:
'- Bloomberg reporting that Oracle and OpenAI pulled out of expanding to these buildings, NVIDIA and Meta might replace them
- Air Permit Application for the buildings, featuring blueprints on p.391 (via Aterio)
- Partnership Announcement with Microsoft

Name: OpenAI Stargate Lordstown
Notes:
The Stargate data center campus in Lordstown, Ohio is one of a set of 5 Stargate sites that were announced in tandem. Current permitting shows plans to retrofit and expand a former General Motors, now Foxconn plant at the site to manufacture data center-related equipment like NVIDIA servers.

We have not seen visual evidence of large data center construction, but we believe there will be a relatively small data center here based on the announcement and subsequent reporting. The capacity of the data center will probably be no more than 0.3 GW, because the announcement stated that the Milam County and Ohio sites could scale to 1.5 GW (combined) by 2027, and the former is planned for 1.2 GW.
Selected Sources:
- Announcement of 5 new Stargate sites mentioning Lordstown
- Water Quality Permitting stating that “manufacturing and assembly” is the main purpose of the construction
- Stormwater Permitting

Name: OpenAI Stargate New Mexico
Notes: The Stargate site in New Mexico (Dona Ana), also known as Project Jupiter, is one of a set of 5 Stargate sites that were announced in tandem. Stormwater permitting documents list a project end date of 2027-12-31, but based on construction progress and the timeline of other Stargate sites, we estimate 2028-12-31. This data center campus will be powered by two microgrids. Air quality permitting pertaining to these two microgrids was used to determine the planned capacity of the campus.
Selected Sources:
- Project Jupiter Website
- Stormwater Permitting
- West Microgrid Permitting
- East Microgrid Permitting

Name: OpenAI Stargate Wisconsin
Notes: The Stargate site in Port Washington, Wisconsin is one of a set of 5 Stargate sites that were announced in tandem. The developer of this site is Vantage, and they state the planned IT load, power density, and other features of the campus on their website. We estimate the date at which the full site is operational based on the environmental analysis filed, which splits the site into two projects. Project 1, which is the first 3 buildings, has a planned completion date of Q3 2028. Project 2, which is the last building, has a planned completion date of Q4 2028.
Selected Sources:
- Data center page on Vantage website
- Environmental Analysis Summary

Name: OpenAI Stargate Michigan
Notes: The Stargate site in Saline, Michigan is one of a set of 5 Stargate sites that were announced in tandem. The developer is Related Digital (which has this project on their website). To estimate the date at which the campus reaches full capacity, we used the date where DTE Energy is expected to ramp up to full capacity, which is reported in permitting. The planned capacity of the site has been reported in various sources, including reports from the Michigan Public Service Commission.
Selected Sources:
- Related Digital website for this site
- Michigan Public Service Commission Energy Contract Approval, reports 1,383 MW as the site capacity
- Air Permitting, calls the site Project Mitten, includes site map
- DTE Electric’s contract approval

Name: OpenAI Stargate Milam
Notes: The Stargate site in Milam County is one of a set of 5 Stargate sites that were announced in tandem. The date that this campus reaches full capacity is currently speculative. However, the planned capacity of the site has been stated by SB Energy, which will provide power for the site. Regulatory filings on TDLR indicate that the first building of the site will be completed October 2026.
Selected Sources:
- Data Center Dynamics article on the site
- SB Energy article, source for 1.2 GW capacity
- TDLR page for the first building of the site, indicating construction completion date

Name: Meta Kuna
Notes: This Idaho site shares its AI-ready design with Meta Temple. Two buildings are planned to start use in summer 2026. While Meta has not publicly announced expansion plans, construction and solar offsets suggest an expansion completed around late 2027.
Selected Sources:
'- Official Pamphlet from Meta
- Application for Power Purchase Agreement, Pleasant Valley Solar 1
- Application for Power Purchase Agreement, Pleasant Valley Solar 2
- Approval to move start date of Solar 2 to May 31, 2026 and staff comments
- Notice of Application for 3rd PPA (Blacks Creek Energy Center)
- Idaho Statesman: Peak construction reached in March 2025
- Idaho Statesman: Expected water usage reported in 2022

Name: Google Kansas City East
Notes: Google Kansas City East is located in Missouri, and has similarities with other Google data centers like Omaha and Columbus. Currently, there is construction evidence of four buildings at the site. We estimate power capacity using satellite imagery of cooling towers. The compute capacity is based on power capacity and the energy efficiency of Google TPU chips that are available when each building becomes operational.
Selected Sources:
- Hunt Midwest Business Center press release linking data center with Google
- DCD article on the site
